name: 1-2-4-All
desc: Engage everyone simultaneously in generating questions, ideas, and suggestions. No one is left out and all contributions matter.
when: When you want to replace a one-way presentation or discussion dominated by a few voices.

phases.label: Alone
phases.instruction: Silently self-reflect on the question. Write down your ideas individually. No talking yet.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Take a quiet moment to yourself. What comes to mind? Jot down your thoughts — there are no wrong answers and no one will judge your notes.

phases.label: Pairs
phases.instruction: Share and develop ideas with one partner. Find common ground or interesting differences.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Find a partner and share what you wrote. Listen as much as you talk. What's new? What builds on your own thinking?

phases.label: Groups of 4
phases.instruction: Two pairs join together. Share the most interesting ideas and build on them.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Bring your pairs together. What did each pair discover? Look for patterns, surprises, and anything you disagree on.

phases.label: All
phases.instruction: Each group shares one key insight or idea with the whole room. Notice patterns and themes.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Each group offers one key idea to the whole room. Keep going until everything important is out — no repeats needed.

tips: Use a crisp, open question that invites genuine reflection
Enforce the individual round — silence is productive
In the All round, ask for one new idea not already shared
structuralInvitation: What is your thinking on [challenge/question]? Please reflect alone first.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: Impromptu Networking
desc: Rapidly share challenges and expectations, building new connections across a group. Sets the tone for open, peer-to-peer collaboration.
when: At the start of a session to energise the group and surface shared challenges quickly.

phases.label: Round 1
phases.instruction: Find a partner you don't know well. Share: what is your biggest challenge with today's topic?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Frame the question clearly: 'In each short conversation, share your honest thinking on [the challenge]. You've got two minutes each direction — go.'

phases.label: Round 2
phases.instruction: New partner. Share: what do you most hope to contribute to today's session?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Find someone you haven't spoken to yet. Share your thinking, listen to theirs. Then move on.

phases.label: Round 3
phases.instruction: New partner. Share: what do you most want to learn or take away from today?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Same again — new person, same question, fresh perspective. Notice how your thinking shifts with each conversation.

phases.label: Debrief
phases.instruction: Return to the whole group. What themes came up? What connections surprised you?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What surprised you? What kept coming up? Did your thinking shift at all after talking to different people?

tips: Keep rounds strictly timed — urgency sparks openness
Encourage people to move across the room, not just turn to their neighbour
Works brilliantly as the very first activity of any session
structuralInvitation: What is the most important challenge you hope we will address today? What do you most hope to contribute?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: Nine Whys
desc: Make the purpose of your work together visible and shared. Surface the deeper motivations that unite a team.
when: When a group needs to reconnect with shared purpose or understand what really drives each member.

phases.label: Pair up
phases.instruction: Find a partner. One person will ask 'why?' repeatedly, the other answers honestly.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Sit with a partner. One of you starts by saying what you do. The other just listens — and gets ready to ask one simple question.

phases.label: The nine whys
phases.instruction: Partner A: describe what you do at work. Partner B: ask 'why is that important to you?' after each answer. Repeat up to 9 times. Listen for what emerges.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Here is your only question for the next few minutes: 'And why is that important to you?' Ask it again after every answer. Don't rush — let the silence work.

phases.label: Switch
phases.instruction: Swap roles. Partner B shares, Partner A asks why repeatedly.
phases.friendlyInstruction: By now you have gone several layers deep. What is the real reason this matters? What showed up that you did not expect?

phases.label: Share themes
phases.instruction: In the whole group: what core purposes emerged? What surprised you about your own answers?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What was it like to be asked 'why' so many times? What surprised you about your own answers? Share the most meaningful thing you discovered.

tips: The questioner should be genuinely curious, not mechanical
Answers often get more honest and surprising after the 4th or 5th why
Notice when someone hits a deep personal truth — those are the important ones
structuralInvitation: What do you do? (then keep asking) And why is that important to you?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by the Toyota "Five Whys" method.

name: Wicked Questions
desc: Articulate the paradoxical challenges that are holding your organisation back. Surface the tensions that everyone knows about but nobody names.
when: When a group is stuck or circling the same problem. When you sense there are unspoken tensions.

phases.label: Generate
phases.instruction: Individually: what are the pairs of opposites that describe your challenge? Start with 'How is it that we...' and 'and at the same time...'
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think about the real tensions in your work — things that feel contradictory but are both completely true. Start writing them as: 'How is it that we [do X] and at the same time [do the opposite]?'

phases.label: Share in small groups
phases.instruction: Share your wicked questions in groups of 4-6. Identify the sharpest, most provocative ones.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Now refine them. Do they feel uncomfortable? Do they capture a REAL tension? The best Wicked Questions make you wince a little — that is a good sign.

phases.label: Gallery share
phases.instruction: Each group shares their sharpest wicked question. Which ones get the biggest reaction? Those are the important ones.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Which question feels most alive — most important AND most uncomfortable? That is usually the one worth staying with as a group.

tips: A good wicked question has no easy answer and both sides are true
The emotional reaction in the room tells you which questions matter most
Don't try to solve the questions — just name them clearly
structuralInvitation: How is it that we [pursue one strategy/value] and at the same time [pursue the exact opposite]?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: Appreciative Interviews
desc: Discover and build on the root causes of success. Interview partners about peak experiences to surface what makes your best work possible.
when: When a group is problem-focused and needs to rediscover its strengths. When you want to build from what works.

phases.label: Prepare questions
phases.instruction: Frame 2-3 questions about peak experiences: when were you most proud of our work? What made it possible? What was your contribution?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think of a moment when this work felt genuinely meaningful — when something clicked. Write 2–3 questions that would help someone else tell that kind of story.

phases.label: Pair interviews
phases.instruction: Interview each other. Listen for the specific conditions and actions that enabled the peak experience. Take notes.
phases.friendlyInstruction: One person asks, the other tells their story. Real listening here — not planning your next question, but actually following where the story goes. Switch after a few minutes.

phases.label: Share stories
phases.instruction: Each person shares their partner's story (not their own). What themes emerge? What conditions keep appearing?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Tell the group what your PARTNER shared, not your own story. What conditions keep coming up across all the stories? What made the best moments possible?

phases.label: Extract lessons
phases.instruction: What conditions and practices can we create more of? What made our best work possible?
phases.friendlyInstruction: If these conditions — the ones that made your best moments happen — could become more common, what would actually need to change? What is one thing you could start doing tomorrow?

tips: Stories of past success are more reliable than abstract ideas about the future
Share your partner's story, not your own — it creates more generous listening
Keep questions grounded in specific experiences, not general opinions
structuralInvitation: Tell me about a time when you were at your best — when you were most proud of your contribution to [shared purpose]. What made it possible?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by Appreciative Inquiry (David Cooperrider).

name: TRIZ
desc: Stop counterproductive activities and behaviours by working backwards from the worst possible outcome. Use 1-2-4-All within each step to surface what everyone knows but nobody says.
when: When a group is stuck doing things they know aren't working. When you need to stop before you can start.

phases.label: Identify the worst outcome
phases.instruction: Introduce the challenge. Invite groups to identify the single most unwanted result — the worst possible outcome.
phases.friendlyInstruction: What would be the absolute worst result here? Name it clearly — that's what we're working backwards from.

phases.label: List what guarantees failure
phases.instruction: Each group uses 1-2-4-All to generate a complete list of everything they could do to guarantee achieving the worst outcome. No filter — the more specific the better.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Using 1-2-4-All: what would you have to do to guarantee that terrible outcome? Be creative and specific. The more outrageous the list, the more honest and useful this becomes.

phases.label: Spot what you're already doing
phases.instruction: Each group uses 1-2-4-All to identify which items from their failure list they are already doing, even if unintentionally. Be honest.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Here's the uncomfortable part. Using 1-2-4-All: which of those failure-guaranteeing things are you actually already doing? Not intentionally — but honestly?

phases.label: Commit to stopping
phases.instruction: Each group uses 1-2-4-All to determine first steps that will help them stop each identified behaviour. What specifically will you stop doing?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Using 1-2-4-All: for each item you identified, what's the first step to stop doing it? Not "we should try to maybe..." — actual commitments. What specifically changes, starting when?

phases.label: Share and debrief
phases.instruction: Groups share their stopping commitments. What surprised us? What was hardest to admit? What is the single most important thing to stop?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What surprised you most? What was hardest to admit out loud? What's the one thing you're most committed to stopping?

tips: Do not accept ideas for starting new things — TRIZ is specifically about stopping. Redirect gently but clearly.
The more outrageous the worst-case list, the more honest and useful the exercise. Encourage exaggeration.
Step 3 (spotting what you're already doing) is where the breakthroughs happen. Make space for uncomfortable truths.
Trap: groups skipping straight to solutions. Hold the "stopping" frame firmly throughout.
Trap: making it abstract. Push for specific named activities and behaviours, not vague concepts.
String with Troika Consulting, Wise Crowds, or Open Space to go deeper on the stopping commitments.
structuralInvitation: Make a complete list of everything you could do to make sure we achieve the worst possible outcome for [challenge].
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by one element of the Russian engineering approach TRIZ (teoriya resheniya izobretatelskikh zadatch).

name: 15% Solutions
desc: Focus on what you can do right now with the resources and authority you already have. Avoid waiting for permission or perfect conditions.
when: When people feel stuck waiting for others to act. When learned helplessness is preventing progress on important challenges.

phases.label: Identify your 15%
phases.instruction: Individually: what is the 15% of the challenge where you have freedom to act? What actions are in your direct control, right now, without needing anyone's permission?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Forget what needs approval, budget, or help from others for now. What could YOU personally do — starting this week — that would make a small but real difference? Write it down.

phases.label: Share in pairs
phases.instruction: Share your 15% with a partner. Help each other see possibilities you might be missing. Your partner asks: what else is within your reach?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Share your idea with your small group. Ask for honest feedback: is this genuinely doable? What might get in the way? How could you make it even more specific?

phases.label: Commit
phases.instruction: Share commitments with a small group. State: I will [action] by [date]. Make it small enough to actually do.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Name your commitment out loud: not 'I'll try to...' but 'I will [specific thing] by [specific date].' Someone write it down — that accountability helps.

tips: 15% is enough to make a real start — perfection is the enemy here
Help people see that their 15% is often larger than they think
The act of stating a commitment publicly dramatically increases follow-through
structuralInvitation: What is your 15%? Where do you have freedom to act right now, without needing anyone's permission or additional resources?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: Troika Consulting
desc: Peers help each other solve real problems using a simple consulting practice. Everyone gets help and everyone gives it.
when: When people have real challenges they need help with and peer wisdom is underutilised.

phases.label: Form trios
phases.instruction: Get into groups of exactly 3. Decide who goes first as the 'client'.
phases.friendlyInstruction: You have got 1–2 minutes. Tell your troika: what is the challenge you are wrestling with right now? Be specific — the more concrete the better. They will just listen for now.

phases.label: Client presents
phases.instruction: The client shares their challenge or question in 2-3 minutes. Consultants listen without interrupting.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Client — turn away or close your eyes. Consultants: what do you think is really going on here? What ideas or questions come to mind? Talk openly — the client is listening but not responding.

phases.label: Consultants confer
phases.instruction: Client turns their back or mutes themselves. The two consultants discuss and generate suggestions, insights, or questions. Client listens silently.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Client — turn back around. What landed for you? What surprised you? What will you take away, even if it was just a word or a reframe?

phases.label: Client reflects
phases.instruction: Client turns back and shares what was most useful. Then rotate — next person becomes the client.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Switch! Each person gets a turn in the client chair. Same energy — be honest, be curious, be generous with each other.

tips: The 'turning away' step is essential — it creates honest consulting conversations
Consultants should ask questions as much as give advice
Rotate so everyone gets to be client at least once
structuralInvitation: What is your most important challenge for which you could use the help and insight of your peers?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: What, So What, Now What? W³
desc: A structured reflection for any group to make sense of shared experiences and move toward action. Uses the Ladder of Inference to distinguish observation from interpretation from commitment.
when: After a significant event, experiment, or period of work. When you need to make sense of what happened.

phases.label: What?
phases.instruction: Individuals work 1 min alone, then 2-7 min in small groups. Ask: "What happened? What did you observe? What facts or data stood out?" Collect salient facts with the whole group (2-3 min). Strictly observable only — no interpretations yet. Note that emotions can be observed as data (e.g. "people were smiling") rather than inferred (e.g. "people were happy").
phases.friendlyInstruction: Start alone for 1 minute: what actually happened? What did you see, hear, or notice? Facts and observations only — things you could have filmed. Then share in small groups, then collect key observations as a whole group. If someone starts interpreting, gently redirect: "That's a great So What — let's hold it for the next round."

phases.label: So What?
phases.instruction: Individuals work 1 min alone, then 2-7 min in small groups. Ask: "Why is that important? What patterns or conclusions are emerging? What hypotheses can you make?" This is the most difficult stage — it's where meaning is made. Collect key themes with the whole group (2-3 min).
phases.friendlyInstruction: Now the harder question: why does it matter? Work alone first (1 min), then in small groups. What patterns do you see in those observations? What does it mean for us? This is the most important stage — don't rush it. Then share the most significant patterns as a whole group.

phases.label: Now What?
phases.instruction: Individuals work 1 min alone, then 2-7 min in small groups. Ask: "What actions make sense now? What will you do differently?" Collect commitments with the whole group.
phases.friendlyInstruction: So what will you actually do? Work alone first (1 min): given what we've observed and what it means, what actions make sense? Then share in small groups and surface the most important commitments to the whole group.

phases.label: Commitments
phases.instruction: Collect specific commitments. Each person or group states what they will do differently. Make commitments public and specific — who does what by when.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Name your commitment out loud — that makes it real. Who does what by when? Vague intentions don't count here. One specific thing from each person or group.

tips: Keep the What? round strictly observational. If someone jumps to interpretation, say: "That's a great So What — let's hold it for the next round."
The So What? is the most challenging stage — resist the urge to skip it. It's where meaning is genuinely made.
The Ladder of Inference (Argyris/Senge) is the theoretical backbone: we climb from data → observations → assumptions → conclusions → actions. W³ slows this climb down.
Trap: collecting a long repetitive list from a single group. Ask for one unique answer at a time from each group.
Trap: emotions being expressed as conclusions ("people were unhappy") rather than observations ("several people left early, two people didn't speak"). Guide participants to observe rather than infer.
For groups of 10-12 or fewer, skip the small group stage and run all three rounds as a whole group.
structuralInvitation: WHAT? What happened? What did you notice, what facts or observations stood out for you?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by the Ladder of Inference (Chris Argyris, 1982; Peter Senge, 1990).

name: Discovery & Action Dialogue
desc: Discover and act on local solutions to seemingly intractable challenges. Six specific questions help groups uncover what is already working locally and spread it — without importing solutions from outside.
when: When a persistent problem has local solutions that haven't been shared. When top-down solutions keep failing.

phases.label: Introduce the six questions
phases.instruction:
The facilitator introduces the six DAD questions that will guide the dialogue. These are fixed — do not skip or modify them:
1. How do you know when [problem] is present?
2. How do you contribute to [problem] occurring, even if unintentionally?
3. What do you do — or could you do — to address [problem]?
4. What gets in the way of doing this all the time?
5. Who else should be involved in creating solutions?
6. What needs to happen to get started?
phases.friendlyInstruction: There are six questions that guide this conversation — and they're fixed for a reason. Each one unlocks a different kind of honest conversation. The facilitator reads them out; take a moment to let them land before we dive in.

phases.label: Small group dialogue
phases.instruction:
In groups of 6-8, work through all six questions. A facilitator guides each group through the sequence:
1. How do you know when [the problem] is present? (observable signs)
2. How do you contribute to this problem, even if unintentionally?
3. What do you do, or could you do, to address the problem?
4. What gets in the way of doing this all the time?
5. Who else should be involved?
6. What needs to happen to get started? What is your first step?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Work through all six questions in your small group. Take your time — especially on question 2 (your own contribution) and question 3 (what actually helps). Question 3 is where the local solutions live. Question 4 is where the real obstacles are. Don't rush past either.

phases.label: Harvest actions
phases.instruction: Each group shares: one thing we will try (a specific action), and one thing we need (support or resource). Make commitments specific and realistic. Record them visibly.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Each group shares two things: one thing you will actually try — specific enough to know if you've done it — and one thing you need to make it possible. No vague intentions. Write them down publicly.

tips: The six questions are the structure. Do not skip or modify them — each one serves a specific purpose.
Question 2 (your own contribution to the problem) is the hardest and most important. Hold the space for honest answers.
Local solutions discovered through DAD are more likely to stick than imported best practices. Celebrate what already works.
Trap: groups jumping to "what should leadership do?" Redirect: "What can YOU do, even in a small way?"
Trap: groups staying abstract. Push for specific, named behaviours and situations.
Follow up at 30 and 60 days to support the actions taken — commitments without follow-up rarely stick.
String with Positive Deviance inquiry or follow with Troika Consulting to support individuals with their first steps.
structuralInvitation: How do you know when [the problem] is present? What do you do that helps, even in small ways? What gets in the way?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by Positive Deviance methods.

name: Shift & Share
desc: Spread the knowledge of innovators and their local solutions across a large group. Replaces lengthy panel presentations.
when: When you have multiple innovators or practitioners with valuable local knowledge to share across a large group.

phases.label: Set up stations
phases.instruction: Innovators set up their stations around the room with materials, examples, or prototypes of their work.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Set up your station with your materials. You will be presenting your work in short bursts to rotating groups — think of it as a mini marketplace of ideas.

phases.label: Round 1
phases.instruction: Groups of 5-8 visit their first station. Innovator shares their work and answers questions.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Visitors arrive. Walk them through your work in 3–5 minutes, then open it up: what questions do they have? What connections do they see? This is a conversation, not a lecture.

phases.label: Round 2
phases.instruction: Groups shift to next station. Fresh presentation, fresh questions.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Rotate! As a visitor, your job is to learn, make connections, and bring ideas back to your own work. What can you adapt?

phases.label: Round 3
phases.instruction: Groups shift to final station.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Reconvene. What was the most interesting thing you saw? What idea from someone else's work could you actually use?

phases.label: Harvest
phases.instruction: Whole group: what are the most transferable insights? What surprised you? What will you try?

tips: Innovators must be willing to share honestly, including failures
Small visiting groups allow real questions, not just polite listening
Works brilliantly at conferences as an alternative to breakout panels
structuralInvitation: What innovative work do you have to share with others that they could adapt and learn from?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: 25/10 Crowd Sourcing
desc: Rapidly generate and rank bold ideas from the whole group. Replaces slow committee processes with energetic crowd wisdom.
when: When you need to generate and prioritise bold ideas from a large group quickly.

phases.label: Write bold ideas
phases.instruction: Everyone writes their boldest idea on a card. Ask: if you had 10x the resources, what would you do?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Write your boldest idea on a card — the one you would pitch if you were 10 times braver than usual. Make it specific enough that someone else could understand and rate it.

phases.label: Pass and score
phases.instruction: Pass cards around randomly. Score each card 1-5 as you receive it. Keep passing every 30 seconds until you've scored 5+ cards.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Swap cards with whoever is near you. Read the card, rate it 1–5 on the back, then swap again. Keep moving — this is fast.

phases.label: Count and reveal
phases.instruction: Add up the scores on your current card. Call out the top scores. The highest 10% are read aloud.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Keep swapping and scoring. You are building a crowd-sourced ranking without anyone knowing whose idea is whose.

phases.label: Debrief
phases.instruction: What surprised you? Which ideas deserve further exploration? What do the top ideas have in common?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Call time. The ideas with the highest total scores bubble up. What are the top ideas? Does anything surprise you about what the crowd valued?

tips: Works best in person with physical cards
The randomness of passing is essential — it removes politics from voting
Top ideas often aren't the obvious ones — trust the process
structuralInvitation: If you were 10 times bolder, what big idea would you recommend we act on? Write your boldest idea on a card.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by Open Space Technology.

name: Wise Crowds
desc: Tap the wisdom of the whole group for each individual's challenges. Everyone gets expert advice from peers; everyone gives it.
when: When individuals have real challenges and the group has relevant collective wisdom that isn't being used.

phases.label: Form groups
phases.instruction: Form groups of 4-5. Each group will work through Troika Consulting so everyone gets a turn as client.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think about the challenge you most need help with right now. Something real — not a hypothetical. Prepare to describe it in about 2 minutes.

phases.label: Rotating Troika rounds
phases.instruction: Each person takes a turn as client (3 min to present challenge), then turns away while the group consults (8 min), then receives feedback (4 min). Rotate until everyone has been client.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Client presents their challenge. Consultants: your job is to listen without preparing your response. Just listen.

phases.label: Synthesis
phases.instruction: Bring the whole group together. What themes appeared across challenges? What collective wisdom emerged? What actions will people take?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Client steps back from the circle. Consultants discuss: what is really going on? What questions would help? What ideas come to mind? Speak freely — the client is listening but silent.

tips: Strictly enforce the rotating structure — don't let one person's challenge dominate
The group consulting without the client present generates more honest, creative ideas
Allow time for written reflection before the group shares back
structuralInvitation: What is your most important challenge for which you could use the collective wisdom of everyone in this room?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: Min Specs
desc: Specify only the absolutely essential rules for achieving a goal and no more. Frees up creativity while ensuring critical constraints are met.
when: When over-specification is stifling creativity, or when you need to define the minimum viable rules for a new initiative.

phases.label: Generate Max Specs
phases.instruction: In the context of a challenging activity, new initiative, or strategic bottleneck, invite participants to generate the complete list of all must-dos and must-not-dos. Work alone for 1 minute, then consolidate and expand in the small group for 5 minutes. Make the list as complete as possible.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think of everything you believe you must do and must never do to achieve your purpose. Start alone (1 min), then share and expand in your small group (5 min). Get everything on the table — don't filter yet. This is your Max Specs list.

phases.label: Test each spec
phases.instruction: Each small group tests every item on their Max Specs list against the purpose statement using this test: "If we broke or ignored this rule, could we still achieve our purpose?" If the answer is yes, the spec is dropped from the list.
phases.friendlyInstruction: For each rule on your list, ask: if we broke this rule, could we still achieve our purpose? If yes — cross it off. Be ruthless. You're looking for the rules that are truly non-negotiable, not just comfortable habits.

phases.label: Second round if needed
phases.instruction: Do a second round of testing if needed. Groups continue challenging each item. Often two to five Min Specs are sufficient — if your list is still longer, keep testing.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Still too many rules on the list? Keep going. The goal is 2-5 truly essential specs — the ones that, if broken, would genuinely prevent you from achieving your purpose. Everything else is optional.

phases.label: Compare and consolidate
phases.instruction: Compare Min Specs across small groups and consolidate to the shortest shared list. Challenge any spec that appears in only one group's list.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Share your minimal lists across groups. Where do you agree? Where does one group have a rule others don't? Challenge those — are they truly essential or just one team's preference?

phases.label: Commit
phases.instruction: Agree on the final minimal set. Announce: everything else is permitted — people are free to innovate within these constraints.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Here are your Min Specs — the true non-negotiables. Everything else is allowed. You now have maximum freedom within minimum constraint. Go wild responsibly.

tips: Groups typically start with 15-20 rules and end with 2-5 real ones. Trust the process even when it feels uncomfortable.
The most important rules are often unwritten and unexamined. Make them explicit before testing them.
Min Specs creates freedom, not chaos. Explain this upfront — people often assume fewer rules means less clarity.
Trap: groups keeping rules out of comfort or habit rather than necessity. Hold the test firmly: "If we broke this, could we still achieve our purpose?"
Trap: confusing Min Specs with best practices. Min Specs are non-negotiable constraints; best practices are recommended approaches.
String with TRIZ to first clear out what you should stop, then use Min Specs to establish the true minimum required.
structuralInvitation: What must we absolutely do and must we absolutely never do to achieve our purpose? Generate a complete list, then reduce it to the true minimum.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: Improv Prototyping
desc: Engage a group to learn and improve rapidly by acting out challenging scenarios and discovering better ways to handle them. Taps three levels of knowledge simultaneously: explicit (what we know), tacit (what we discover by watching each other), and latent (new ideas that emerge through improvisation).
when: When a group faces a frustrating recurring challenge and needs to discover better behaviours — not just discuss them. When training, retrospectives, or talk-based approaches have stopped producing new insight.

phases.label: Explain and set the stage
phases.instruction: Explain what will be done and describe the sequence of steps (2 min). Set the stage by describing the scenario that will be acted out and the various roles (3 min). Invite 2-4 players to come forward.
phases.friendlyInstruction: First, walk everyone through what's about to happen — no surprises. Then set the scene: what's the situation we're going to play out? Who are the characters? Invite a few brave volunteers to come forward as the first players.

phases.label: Enact the scene
phases.instruction: Players on stage enact the scene (3-5 min). The scene should depict the challenge as it actually happens — not an idealised version. Other participants observe in small groups, each group looking for specific things.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Players: just act it out as it really happens. Don't try to show the ideal version — show the real one, warts and all. Observers: watch closely. What's working, even slightly? What's definitely not working? Your group will discuss this in a moment.
phases.groups: Players on stage; observers in small groups

phases.label: Small group debrief
phases.instruction: Each small observer group uses 1-2-4-All to identify successful and unsuccessful "chunks" from the scene they just observed. What specific moments or behaviours worked? What didn't?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Using 1-2-4-All in your small group: what were the specific moments that worked — even slightly? What clearly didn't? You're looking for concrete chunks of behaviour, not general impressions.

phases.label: Build improved prototype
phases.instruction: Each observer group pieces together the successful chunks into a new prototype. Volunteers from within the group act out the new prototype for their own small group only (not the whole room yet). Refine it.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Your group now builds an improved version by keeping what worked and replacing what didn't. Act it out among yourselves — not for the whole room yet. Try things. Laugh. Adjust. What's better now?

phases.label: Share best prototype
phases.instruction: Participants from the group with the most improved prototype volunteer to come on stage and enact their version for the whole group (3-5 min). Continue with as many rounds as needed to arrive at one or more prototypes good enough to put into practice.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Which group has a version they're proud of? Come on stage and show us. The rest of us watch: what's genuinely better? What else could change? Keep going until you have something worth actually trying in real life.

tips: Stay close to a real challenge the group is actually facing. Invented scenarios feel safe but generate less insight.
Encourage exaggeration — it makes patterns visible and keeps the atmosphere playful while doing serious work.
Trap: groups getting stuck talking about the scene rather than acting it. Redirect: "Let's see it — start acting."
Trap: people feeling uncomfortable with roleplay. Normalise it early; give people supporting roles (director, stage manager) if they're hesitant to perform.
Invite creative swaps: doctor plays nurse, manager plays team member. Perspective shifts unlock new insight.
String with Discovery & Action Dialogue or Simple Ethnography to source the scenarios, and Design StoryBoards or Shift & Share to spread the innovations.
structuralInvitation: What frustrating recurring challenge would benefit from being acted out so we can discover better ways to handle it together?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by Antonas Mockus (former mayor of Bogota) and Improv artists.

name: Helping Heuristics
desc: Practise and develop four progressive methods for helping others: Expert, Doctor, Process Consultant, and Facilitator. Avoid the common trap of jumping straight to advice before understanding what is truly needed.
when: When people default to giving advice before understanding. When helping relationships feel unequal or ineffective.

phases.label: Introduce the four positions
phases.instruction:
Introduce and demonstrate the four helping positions:
1. Expert — you give information or do the task for them
2. Doctor — you diagnose, then prescribe a solution
3. Process Consultant — you help them solve their own problem, without telling them what to do
4. Facilitator — you help the group help itself
Each position is appropriate in different situations. The trap is defaulting to Expert or Doctor when a different position would serve better.
phases.friendlyInstruction:
There are four ways to help someone, and most of us default to the first two:
1. Expert — "here's the answer"
2. Doctor — "here's what's wrong, here's my prescription"
3. Process Consultant — "let me help you figure it out yourself"
4. Facilitator — "let me help the group figure it out together"
Watch a demonstration of each. Notice when each position is most useful — and when it's overused.

phases.label: Practise in trios
phases.instruction: In groups of 3: one person brings a real challenge (client), one practices helping (helper), one observes (observer). The helper must start with curiosity — asking questions to understand before offering anything. Try to move through the positions progressively, only advancing when the previous position is insufficient. Observer gives specific feedback after.
phases.friendlyInstruction: In trios: one person brings a real challenge, one tries to help, one watches. Helper: you must start with genuine curiosity. Ask questions. Resist the urge to jump to Expert or Doctor mode immediately. Move through the positions only as needed. Observer: give honest, specific feedback on what you saw.

phases.label: Rotate roles
phases.instruction: Rotate so each person gets a turn in each role. Switch client and helper at minimum.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Switch roles. Everyone should experience being the client, the helper, and the observer.

phases.label: Debrief
phases.instruction: Debrief as a whole group: Which position did helpers default to? When was each position most useful? What was hardest about holding back advice? What will you do differently in your next helping conversation?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What position did helpers default to? What was hardest about staying curious before jumping to answers? When was the Expert position actually the right one? What changes in how you'll help people going forward?

tips: Most helpers jump straight to Expert or Doctor. Name this pattern explicitly and without judgment before starting.
The best help often comes after two minutes of the helper saying nothing — just listening and asking questions.
Trap: helpers giving advice while calling it "process consulting." Watch for advice dressed up as questions ("Have you tried...?").
Trap: clients not bringing real challenges. Encourage genuine problems, not hypotheticals.
String with Troika Consulting for a deeper peer-helping experience, or follow with 15% Solutions to action the insights.
structuralInvitation: How can you help someone without taking over? What does the person in front of you actually need from you right now?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by Edgar Schein's work on helping relationships.

name: Conversation Café
desc: Engage everyone in making sense of challenging, complex, or controversial topics. Creates conditions for genuine dialogue rather than debate.
when: When a topic is genuinely complex or controversial and people need to think together, not just argue.

phases.label: Check-in
phases.instruction: Each person shares one word or sentence in response to the central question. No discussion yet.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Welcome everyone. The question we are exploring together today is [question]. There are a few simple agreements: listen to understand, speak from your own experience, and allow silence.

phases.label: Round 1 — open
phases.instruction: Open dialogue. Anyone can speak. Focus on understanding each other, not winning arguments. Use a talking object if helpful.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Round 1: everyone speaks once on the question, briefly. No discussion yet — just your initial take. The talking piece moves around the circle.

phases.label: Round 2 — deeper
phases.instruction: Deepen the conversation. What assumptions are we making? What are we not saying? What would change our minds?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Round 2 and 3: now it opens up. Build on what others have said, change your mind, share what struck you. The talking piece still goes to whoever holds it.

phases.label: Harvest
phases.instruction: What have we learned? What questions remain open? What surprised us? Share with the whole group.
phases.friendlyInstruction: What themes and insights emerged? What surprised you? What question are you leaving with that you did not arrive with?

phases.label: Check-out
phases.instruction: One word or sentence each: what are you leaving with?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What will you take away from this conversation? Not conclusions — just what shifted or opened up for you.

tips: A talking object (pen, stone) can help regulate airtime in heated conversations
Seat people in circles, not rows
The facilitator's job is to protect the space, not direct the content
structuralInvitation: What do you think about [complex topic]? What meaning does it have for you?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: User Experience Fishbowl
desc: Elicit authentic user experience stories to reveal what's working and what isn't from the people who actually use your service.
when: When you need real user stories to inform design or improvement. When experts are disconnected from users' actual experience.

phases.label: Set up fishbowl
phases.instruction: 4-6 users sit in the centre. Everyone else observes from outside. A facilitator manages the inner group.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Welcome our users into the inner circle. Everyone else forms the outer circle. A simple reminder: the inner circle speaks, the outer circle listens — really listens, without preparing rebuttals.

phases.label: Storytelling
phases.instruction: Users tell their stories: what happened when you tried to use our service? The facilitator asks gentle follow-up questions. Observers take notes in silence.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Users: tell us your real experience. What happens when you try to use this service? Walk us through it — the good, the confusing, the frustrating. We are here to learn, not defend.

phases.label: Observer questions
phases.instruction: Observers can enter the inner circle to ask one question each, then return to the outside. No debate, no defence.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Outer circle: now you speak. What did you hear that surprised you? What did you not know? What questions are you sitting with? No explanations — just what you learned.

tips: Observers must not interrupt or defend — this is the hardest rule to enforce
The discomfort experts feel hearing real stories is productive
Follow up with What, So What, Now What? to process what was heard
structuralInvitation: What happens when you try to use our service? Tell us your story — what did you experience?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: Heard, Seen, Respected
desc: Practice the empathic listening and responding that builds trust. Surface experiences of not being heard, seen, or respected without blame.
when: When trust is low or has been damaged. When people feel unheard. Before important or difficult conversations.

phases.label: Story sharing
phases.instruction: In pairs: share a time when you did not feel heard, seen, or respected. Speak from personal experience. The listener only listens — no fixing, no sharing their own story.
phases.friendlyInstruction: We are going to share stories from our own experience. The rule is simple: speak in the first person. Share a time when YOU did not feel heard, seen, or respected. No 'we' statements, no general observations — just your story.

phases.label: Reflection
phases.instruction: Listener reflects back what they heard: 'I heard you say...' Check: did you feel heard? Switch roles.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Pairs share their stories. The listener's only job is to listen — no advice, no 'that happened to me too,' no fixing. Just be with the story.

phases.label: Groups of 4
phases.instruction: Two pairs join. What did you notice about listening? What made someone feel heard or not heard?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Back in the full group: what patterns emerged across the stories? Not to solve them — just to name them. What did it feel like to be truly listened to?

phases.label: Whole group
phases.instruction: What conditions create genuine listening? What will you do differently in your conversations going forward?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What does this experience tell us about how we show up for each other day to day? What is one thing you want to do differently?

tips: The listener must not share their own story during step 1 — this is surprisingly hard
Stories of not being heard are universal — expect recognition and emotion
This structure alone builds more trust than most team-building exercises
structuralInvitation: Share a time when you did not feel heard, seen, or respected. Tell your story from your own experience.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: Drawing Together
desc: Enable people to communicate complex ideas with simple drawings. Bypasses the limitations of language and surfaces hidden assumptions.
when: When language alone isn't revealing enough, or when a group needs to think together about something complex or emotional.

phases.label: Draw your challenge
phases.instruction: Draw your current understanding of the challenge. No writing allowed. Stick figures welcome. Use only basic symbols: circle, triangle, square, star, person, arrow.
phases.friendlyInstruction: You will draw your response to the challenge — but there is a rule: you can only use these five shapes: circle, triangle, square, star, and a simple person. No words allowed. Start now.

phases.label: Gallery and narrate
phases.instruction: Pin drawings up. Each person narrates their drawing in 60 seconds. Others listen without comment.
phases.friendlyInstruction: In your small group, each person shares what they drew and what it means. Look for shared themes or symbols that appeared independently.

phases.label: Patterns
phases.instruction: What patterns appear across the drawings? What do the differences tell us? What assumptions are visible?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What patterns do you see across the drawings? What does the visual language reveal that words might have hidden or complicated?

tips: Explicitly say there's no skill required — reassure people early
Drawings reveal mental models that words hide
Pay attention to what's missing from the drawings as much as what's there
structuralInvitation: Draw your response to the challenge using only these basic shapes: a circle, a triangle, a square, a star, and a simple person. No writing allowed.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: Design StoryBoards
desc: Describe the desired user experience in detail before building solutions. Makes the invisible visible.
when: When designing new services, products, or processes. When you want to centre the user before jumping to solutions.

phases.label: Set the scene
phases.instruction: Who is the user? What is the context? Define the character and situation together as a group.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think about your user — someone specific, not a persona. What does a genuinely great experience look like for them? Start drawing the first moment of their journey.

phases.label: Draw the journey
phases.instruction: In small teams, sketch the user's experience step by step. What do they see, feel, do at each stage? No writing — only drawings.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Continue the storyboard: what happens next? What do they see, feel, and do at each step? Keep drawing — stick figures are fine, this is not about art.

phases.label: Gallery walk
phases.instruction: Post storyboards on the wall. Everyone walks around and leaves sticky note reactions — stars for what's valuable, questions for what's unclear.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Share your storyboard with another pair. What is working? Where does the experience break down? What did the drawing reveal that a written plan would have hidden?

phases.label: Synthesise
phases.instruction: What common elements appear across storyboards? What's the essential experience we're designing for?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What is the single most important moment in your storyboard — the one that makes or breaks the whole experience? What would it take to get that moment right?

tips: Stick figures are perfect — this isn't an art competition
Focus on the user's emotional journey, not just the steps
The gallery walk generates more ideas than the drawing phase
structuralInvitation: What does a great experience look like for [user]? Draw it step by step — what do they see, feel, and do at each moment?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: Celebrity Interview
desc: Elicit wisdom from an expert or leader in a way that is honest, engaging, and avoids the prepared-speech trap. Makes tacit knowledge explicit.
when: When an expert or leader has valuable experience to share and a traditional Q&A feels too formal or shallow.

phases.label: Prepare questions
phases.instruction: Audience generates questions using 1-2-4-All. Focus on: what would we most want to know? What would we be afraid to ask?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Introduce your guest to the group. Set the context: why is this person's experience and perspective valuable right now?

phases.label: The interview
phases.instruction: A skilled interviewer draws out the guest's experience with the audience's best questions. Go where the energy goes — don't stick to a script.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Interview your guest with open, curious questions. Think of yourself as a genuinely interested journalist — not a moderator. Follow the interesting threads, not your script.

phases.label: Debrief
phases.instruction: What surprised us? What was most useful? What questions remain?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Open it up: what questions does the wider group have? What do they want to know that the interview did not cover?

tips: The interviewer makes or breaks this — choose someone who listens well and asks follow-ups
Encourage the guest to share failures and doubts, not just successes
Audience-generated questions are always better than pre-prepared ones
structuralInvitation: Tell me about a time when [your work on this topic] went particularly well. What made it possible?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: Social Network Webbing
desc: Visualise how a community or organisation is connected. Identify brokers, bridges, and gaps to spread innovation and build resilience.
when: When you need to understand how information and influence actually flow — not how the org chart says they do.

phases.label: Map the network
phases.instruction: Place name cards on a large surface. Draw connections between people who regularly share information or collaborate. Use different colours for strong/weak ties.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think about how your work actually gets done — not the org chart, but the real flow. Who do you actually go to for information, advice, or to get things moving? Write their names.

phases.label: Analyse
phases.instruction: Where are the bridges (connectors between clusters)? Where are the silos? Who is over-connected and at risk of burnout?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Map your connections on the shared diagram. Draw lines to the people you named. Thick lines for frequent contact, thin for occasional.

phases.label: Plan
phases.instruction: What connections need to be strengthened? What new bridges need to be built? What structures are creating silos?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Look at the map together. Where are the hubs — the people everything flows through? Where are the gaps — places where connection is missing but needed? What surprises you?

tips: Let the group map it themselves — they know the real connections
Bridges are often the most valuable and most overlooked people
Silos often exist for reasons — explore them with curiosity, not blame
structuralInvitation: Who do you regularly turn to for information, advice, or collaboration to get your work done? Let's map the real connections.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: What I Need From You (WINFY)
desc: People in different functions or groups can quickly improve how they ask each other for what they need to succeed. By articulating core needs clearly and receiving unambiguous responses (yes, no, I will try, or whatever), you boost clarity, integrity, and transparency while dissolving misunderstandings that have built up across silos.
when: When groups or teams depend on each other but have accumulated vague resentments or unarticulated needs. When you need to replace complaints with concrete requests. When cross-functional coordination is breaking down.

phases.label: Brief and set context
phases.instruction: Reiterate the shared goal or challenge being addressed — context must be the same for all. Explain the four permitted responses: YES, NO, I WILL TRY, and WHATEVER (meaning: your request was too vague to answer specifically). No other responses are allowed. No elaboration or discussion when responding. Position the functional clusters around the room.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Before we start, let's be clear about the shared goal we're all working toward. Then the rules: when you respond to a request, you can only say YES, NO, I WILL TRY, or WHATEVER — which means "your request was too vague for me to give you a real answer." No explanations. No negotiation. No "but what I meant was..." Clear requests get clear answers.

phases.label: Craft top needs
phases.instruction: Functional clusters use 1-2-4-All to identify their top needs from each of the other groups in the room. Needs are expressed as requests in the form: "What I need from you is ____." Each cluster reduces their list to their two most important needs, writes them down in exact form, and selects a spokesperson.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Using 1-2-4-All in your group: what do you truly need from each of the other groups here in order to succeed? Complete the sentence: "What I need from you is ___." Be specific — vague requests get "WHATEVER" as an answer. Reduce to your two most important needs per group, write them down precisely, and choose your spokesperson.

phases.label: Express needs — no responses yet
phases.instruction: Spokespeople come to the centre (inner circle). Each spokesperson reads their needs to the relevant groups. Receiving groups ONLY LISTEN — no responses yet, no reactions. This is strictly enforced.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Spokespeople: come to the centre. Each of you will read your needs aloud to the groups you're addressing. Everyone else: your only job right now is to listen. No reactions, no nodding, no "mmm". Strictly listen. You'll get to respond shortly.
phases.groups: Spokespeople in inner circle; others listen

phases.label: Consult and formulate responses
phases.instruction: Spokespeople return to their groups. Each group confers and decides on their response to each request directed at them: YES, NO, I WILL TRY, or WHATEVER. No other options. Groups select their response and prepare to deliver it without elaboration.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Spokespeople: go back to your groups. Discuss the requests directed at you and decide: YES, NO, I WILL TRY, or WHATEVER. Choose one — you cannot negotiate or qualify it when you deliver it. No "it depends" or "we would if..." Just the answer.

phases.label: Deliver responses
phases.instruction: Spokespeople reconvene in the inner circle. Each spokesperson delivers their responses to each request, one at a time, without elaboration. No discussion allowed at this stage.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Spokespeople: back to the centre. Deliver your answers one by one — just the answer, nothing more. No explaining. No softening. The directness is the point.
phases.groups: Spokespeople in inner circle; others listen

phases.label: Debrief
phases.instruction: Debrief with What, So What, Now What? What surprised us? What requests got "WHATEVER" — what does that tell us about how clearly we express our needs? What commitments emerged? What was not asked that should have been?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What surprised you? Which responses were unexpected? Any "WHATEVER" answers — what does that tell you about how clearly you're asking? What commitments did you hear that you want to hold each other to? And: what did you need but didn't ask for?

tips: Emphasise that requests must be specific enough to receive a yes or no. Vague requests always get "WHATEVER" — and that's the point.
Strictly enforce "no immediate response" when needs are first expressed. Rushing to respond before consulting the group undermines the process.
Strictly enforce the four-response rule. No elaboration, no negotiation. This directness is what makes WINFY work.
Trap: groups expressing complaints rather than requests. Redirect: "How can you turn that into a specific request?"
Trap: "I WILL TRY" being used to avoid a real yes or no. Consider replacing it with "HUH?" (Keith McCandless's suggestion) — more lovingly provocative.
This structure can surface real tension. Keep the atmosphere warm and constructive without undermining the seriousness.
String with Nine Whys beforehand to deepen purpose clarity, or debrief with What, So What, Now What? to process what emerged.
structuralInvitation: What I need from you to be successful in achieving [shared goal] is... (complete the sentence with a clear, specific request)
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by Kathie Dannemiller and professor Dan Pesut.

name: Open Space Technology
desc: Invite a group to create and manage its own agenda around a single, complex theme. Radical self-organisation at scale.
when: When the challenge is complex, the people who need to contribute are diverse, and commitment to action is required.

phases.label: Opening circle
phases.instruction: Gather the whole group in a circle. The convener introduces the theme and the four principles: whoever comes is the right people, whenever it starts is the right time, whatever happens is the only thing that could, when it's over it's over.
phases.friendlyInstruction: If you have a question or topic you feel genuinely passionate about and personally responsible for exploring — come up and post it. You are voting with your feet when you choose sessions.

phases.label: Marketplace
phases.instruction: Anyone who wants to convene a session announces it and posts it on the wall. Sessions self-organise into available spaces and times.
phases.friendlyInstruction: You have your marketplace of sessions. The Law of Two Feet applies: if you are not learning or contributing, move. No guilt — movement is encouraged.

phases.label: Sessions
phases.instruction: Concurrent self-organised sessions run in parallel. Participants vote with their feet — move if you're not learning or contributing. Conveners take notes.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Work is happening in parallel sessions right now. Follow your energy. Take notes on what emerges — the harvest comes at the end.

phases.label: Evening news
phases.instruction: Brief summaries of what happened in each session. What insights are emerging?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Each session host shares the key insights, questions, and commitments from their group. What will we actually do with what was discovered?

phases.label: Closing circle
phases.instruction: What are we taking away? What actions will we commit to? What remains open?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Looking at everything that emerged today: what surprised you? What feels most important? What are you now committed to?

tips: The 'law of two feet' is essential — people must feel free to move
Trust the process even when it looks chaotic — it always self-organises
Requires a skilled convener but minimal facilitation once started
structuralInvitation: What questions or issues do you most care about, for which you have genuine passion and real responsibility to act?
attribution: Developed by Harrison Owen. Included in the Liberating Structures repertoire by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: Generative Relationships STAR
desc: Assess and build the qualities of relationships that are needed for complex collaboration. Uses four dimensions: Separate, Tuning, Acting, Reciprocating.
when: When a team or partnership needs to reflect honestly on how they're working together and what needs to improve.

phases.label: Assess individually
phases.instruction: Each person rates the relationship on four dimensions: Separate (maintaining identity), Tuning (paying attention), Acting (taking initiative), Reciprocating (giving back). Use 1-5 scale.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Look at the four STAR dimensions together: Separateness, Tuning, Acting Together, and Reason to Return. Without judging, where would you honestly place your relationship on each one right now?

phases.label: Share and compare
phases.instruction: Share your ratings and reasoning. Where do you agree? Where do you see things differently? Explore the differences with curiosity.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Share your ratings and the reasoning behind them. Where do you agree? Where do you see it differently? The gaps are where the most interesting conversation lives.

phases.label: Plan
phases.instruction: What small shifts in each dimension would make this relationship more generative? What will you each do differently?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Given where you are on the STAR dimensions, what would move this relationship in a more generative direction? What is one specific thing each party could do differently?

tips: Honest self-assessment is more valuable than polite agreement
Differences in perception are data, not problems
Focus on small, concrete behavioural changes rather than big abstract commitments
structuralInvitation: How generative is our relationship? Where do we stand on each of the four STAR dimensions: Separateness, Tuning, Acting Together, Reason to Return?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by the work of Glenda Eoyang.

name: Agreement-Certainty Matrix
desc: Identify which kind of challenge you're facing and choose the appropriate response. Distinguishes simple, complicated, complex, and chaotic challenges.
when: When a group is using the wrong approach for its type of challenge — for example, applying planning to complexity.

phases.label: Map your challenges
phases.instruction: List your current challenges and place each in one of four zones based on agreement and certainty levels among stakeholders.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Look at the challenge you are facing. On the matrix: how much genuine agreement is there among the people involved? And how certain are you about what causes what? Place it honestly.

phases.label: Discuss placements
phases.instruction: Share your mappings. Where do you disagree about where a challenge belongs? The disagreements are often the most important conversations.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Compare where different people placed the same challenge. Where do you disagree about the level of agreement or certainty? That disagreement is itself important information.

phases.label: Match approaches
phases.instruction: For each zone, what's the right approach? Simple = best practice. Complicated = good practice. Complex = emergent practice. Chaotic = novel practice.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Given where your challenges sit on the matrix, what kind of approach actually fits? Simple problems need different methods than complex or chaotic ones — what does your placement suggest?

tips: Most groups apply complicated-zone solutions to complex-zone problems — this is a common and expensive mistake
Disagreement about which zone a challenge belongs in is productive, not a problem to resolve quickly
Use this before planning processes to ensure you're using the right planning approach
structuralInvitation: Where does each of your current challenges fall on the matrix? How much agreement is there among stakeholders, and how much certainty do you have about cause and effect?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by the Cynefin framework (Dave Snowden).

name: Simple Ethnography
desc: Observe and record the actual experience of users or front-line workers. Make the gap between how work is imagined and how it actually happens visible.
when: When decision-makers are disconnected from how work actually happens. When you suspect the real story is different from the official story.

phases.label: Prepare
phases.instruction: What will you observe? What questions will guide your observation? Prepare an observation guide. Remember: you are there to learn, not to fix.
phases.friendlyInstruction: We are going out to observe, not to evaluate. Your job is to watch and listen as a genuine learner — not as an expert, not as an auditor. Note what you actually see and hear, not what you expect.

phases.label: Observe
phases.instruction: Visit the workplace or service point. Observe and take notes. Watch what people actually do, not what they say they do. Take photos if permitted.
phases.friendlyInstruction: In the field: observe without intervening. Write down specific moments, quotes, and details — not your interpretations of them. What is actually happening?

phases.label: Debrief with host
phases.instruction: Thank your hosts. Share one observation that surprised you. Ask what they wish decision-makers knew about their work.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Back together: share your raw observations first, before any interpretation. What did you see? What did you hear? What did you not expect?

phases.label: Sense-making
phases.instruction: With your partner: what did you observe? So what does it mean? Now what should change?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Now interpret: what do the observations mean? What do they suggest about how things actually work, as opposed to how we assumed they worked?

tips: Arrive as a learner, not an expert or inspector
The most interesting data is often what surprises you
Always share back what you learned — don't just extract information
structuralInvitation: What is really happening when people do this work? Let's go observe for ourselves — as learners, not as inspectors.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by ethnographic research traditions in social science.

name: Integrated~Autonomy
desc: Move from either/or to both/and thinking on the tension between local autonomy and central coordination. Develop strategies that honor both.
when: When a group is stuck arguing between centralisation and decentralisation, standardisation and flexibility.

phases.label: Generate both sides
phases.instruction: In two parallel groups: one generates all the reasons for more autonomy, the other for more integration. Be bold — don't self-censor.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think about what you need that is more locally controlled AND more centrally coordinated — simultaneously. Not a compromise, but genuinely both. Write down the tensions you are holding.

phases.label: Share and listen
phases.instruction: Each group presents. The other group listens for what's genuinely valuable in the opposing view. No debate yet.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Using 1-2-4-All, explore: what would enable more local autonomy without losing the benefits of integration? And more integration without losing local ownership?

phases.label: Both/And
phases.instruction: Together: what strategies would give us more of both? What conditions enable both autonomy and integration at the same time?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What specific structures, agreements, or practices could make both more possible at the same time? What would you need to stop doing to make room?

phases.label: Commit
phases.instruction: What specific changes will you make? Which practices enable both dimensions simultaneously?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Name your commitments: what will you actually do to move toward integration AND autonomy? Who needs to be involved that is not in this room?

tips: The 'listening for value' step is critical — it breaks the debate dynamic
Both/And solutions exist for most autonomy-integration tensions — trust the process
This works for any either/or dilemma, not just autonomy vs integration
structuralInvitation: What do we need that provides both more local autonomy AND more central integration simultaneously? What would make both more possible at the same time?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.

name: Critical Uncertainties
desc: Develop strategies for operating in a highly uncertain environment by exploring scenarios created from your most critical unknowns.
when: When planning in genuinely uncertain environments. When you need robust strategies that work across multiple possible futures.

phases.label: Identify uncertainties
phases.instruction: Generate all the important uncertainties facing your initiative. Reduce to the 2 most critical — those with highest impact and highest uncertainty.
phases.friendlyInstruction: List all the factors that could significantly affect the success of your work. Now identify the two that are both most impactful AND most uncertain. These are your critical uncertainties.

phases.label: Create scenario matrix
phases.instruction: Place your two critical uncertainties on a 2x2 matrix. Name each of the four scenarios that results.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Plot your two critical uncertainties on a 2×2 matrix. This creates four possible future scenarios. Give each scenario a name that captures its essential character.

phases.label: Explore scenarios
phases.instruction: In small groups, deeply explore each scenario. What would the world look like? What would be true? What would you need to do?
phases.friendlyInstruction: For each scenario: what would it mean for your work? What would you need to do differently? What early signals would tell you this scenario was emerging?

phases.label: Robust strategies
phases.instruction: Which strategies work across multiple scenarios? What are the early indicators that tell you which scenario is unfolding?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Looking across all four scenarios: what strategies would serve you well in any of them? What would you do right now that makes sense regardless of which future unfolds?

tips: The choice of the two critical uncertainties determines everything — take time to get this right
Good scenarios feel uncomfortable — if all four feel manageable, the uncertainties aren't critical enough
Look for 'no-regret' actions that make sense in all scenarios
structuralInvitation: What are the most critical unknowns — the factors with the highest impact and highest uncertainty — that could most affect the success of our work?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by scenario planning methods.

name: Ecocycle Planning
desc: Analyse the full portfolio of activities and initiatives. Identify what to grow, what to let go, and where creative destruction is needed.
when: For strategic planning when a group needs to honestly assess all its current activities and make resource decisions.

phases.label: Map activities
phases.instruction: List all current activities, projects, and initiatives on sticky notes. Then place each on the Ecocycle map: Birth, Maturity, Creative Destruction, or Renewal.
phases.friendlyInstruction: List all your current activities and initiatives. Now honestly: where is each one in its lifecycle? Growing? Mature and efficient? Declining? Stuck? Place each one on the Ecocycle map.

phases.label: Identify traps
phases.instruction: Where are you trapped in a Poverty Trap (nothing new starting) or Rigidity Trap (can't let go of mature activities)? Mark these.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Look at your map: where is the Creative Destruction needed — what needs to end or radically transform so something new can begin? What are you keeping alive out of habit, not value?

phases.label: Whole group review
phases.instruction: Share your maps. What patterns do you see across the organisation? Where are most resources going?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Where are the opportunities for renewal? What new initiatives or approaches are waiting in the wings? What would need to stop for them to have space?

phases.label: Plan moves
phases.instruction: What will you start? What will you stop? What will you accelerate? Make specific decisions.
phases.friendlyInstruction: What specific decisions follow from this map? What will you stop, start, or fundamentally change? Name them.

phases.label: Commitments
phases.instruction: Each team commits to specific moves: at least one stop and one start.
phases.friendlyInstruction: How will you revisit this map? The Ecocycle is a living picture, not a one-time exercise — what is your commitment to using it?

tips: The creative destruction quadrant is the hardest — naming what to stop is courageous work
Include informal activities, not just official projects
Pair with TRIZ to go deeper on the stopping side
structuralInvitation: Where are each of your current activities and initiatives in their natural lifecycle? Place each one on the Ecocycle map.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by the Ecocycle model (C.S. Holling) and Panarchy theory.

name: Panarchy
desc: Understand how actions and interactions at one scale of your system affect other scales. Plan across multiple levels simultaneously.
when: When local actions have unintended consequences at larger scales, or when global changes are creating local problems.

phases.label: Map the levels
phases.instruction: Identify the different scales of your system (individual, team, department, organisation, ecosystem). What is happening at each level?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think about the different scales of your system: what is happening at the individual level? The team level? The organisation level? The wider ecosystem? Describe what you see at each level.

phases.label: Identify connections
phases.instruction: How do actions at one level affect other levels? Where are the 'revolt' connections (small disrupting large) and 'remember' connections (large constraining small)?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Where do the scales interact? How do actions at one level ripple through the others — up and down? Where is the leverage? Where are the traps?

phases.label: Plan across scales
phases.instruction: What actions at which level would have the most leverage? How can you act locally while thinking at multiple scales?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Given what you see, what actions at which level would have the most positive impact across the whole system? What would you stop doing at one level to create room at another?

tips: This is a complex framework — introduce it briefly before diving in
The connections between levels are often more important than any single level
Resist the urge to only plan at the level you control
structuralInvitation: What is happening at different scales of your system — individual, team, organisation, wider ecosystem? How do these levels interact and influence each other?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by Panarchy theory (Holling, Gunderson, and Allen).

name: Purpose-to-Practice
desc: Define the five essential elements needed to create a resilient, enduring initiative: Purpose, Principles, Participants, Structure, and Practices.
when: When launching a new initiative, team, or organisation and you need shared foundations from the start.

phases.label: Purpose
phases.instruction: What is our deepest aspiration? What are we here to do? Use 1-2-4-All to craft a purpose statement everyone believes in.
phases.friendlyInstruction: What is our deepest aspiration — our purpose? Not a mission statement. The real reason this work matters. Take your time with this one.

phases.label: Principles
phases.instruction: What values and principles will guide all our decisions? Use Min Specs thinking — only include what's truly essential.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Given that purpose, what principles will guide how we work together? Not rules — the values that should show up in our day-to-day decisions.

phases.label: Participants
phases.instruction: Who must be included? Who are all the stakeholders? What are their roles? Who is missing from this conversation?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Who needs to be part of this? Who has a stake in the purpose and brings something essential? Think beyond the obvious people.

phases.label: Structure
phases.instruction: How will we organise ourselves? What decisions can be made by whom? How will we communicate?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What specific structures will enable the purpose? Meetings, roles, decision-making processes — what needs to exist and how should it work?

phases.label: Practices
phases.instruction: What specific activities and routines will bring our purpose to life? What will we do differently starting tomorrow?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What practices will we commit to? The regular activities that keep us connected to the purpose and accountable to each other?

tips: Work through each element in order — they build on each other
Return to Purpose whenever discussions get stuck
Document outputs carefully — these are your founding documents
structuralInvitation: What is our deepest aspiration — our purpose? Why does this work matter, and what must we do to bring it to life?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
